documents.body: An agent system's execution path defines how work moves from an initial condition to a terminal outcome. It is separate from the decision logic inside an individual node. A state machine may control the outer workflow while one state uses bounded ReAct, and a fixed pipeline may contain a planning stage. Architecture reviews should therefore name both the outer execution structure and the node-level decision method instead of forcing the whole system under one label.
documents.description: Defines Direct, Pipeline, Router, State Machine, and DAG execution structures plus their cross-cutting production controls.

A state machine may control the outer workflow while one state uses bounded ReAct, and a fixed pipeline may contain a planning stage. Architecture r…
documents.title: Agent execution paths

documents.description: Defines Direct, Pipeline, Router, State Machine, and DAG execution structures plus their cross-cutting production controls.
documents.excerpt: Every execution structure should define: - trace and correlation identifiers; - persisted state when interruption or recovery is possible; - typed retryable, permanent, policy, and user errors; - timeout and cancellation behavior; - bounded retry with backoff; - idempotency for side effects; - fallback and escalation…
documents.title: Agent execution paths

documents.body:
A direct path uses one bounded operation when the information, tools, and output contract are already known. It may still include input validation, one predetermined tool call, an output schema, policy checks, timeouts, and deterministic post-processing.

Direct is the strongest baseline when a single operation can solve the task because it minimizes moving parts, latency, cost, and failure ambiguity. Adding planners, critics, or loops to a bounded transformation should require evidence that they improve quality enough to justify the larger failure surface.
documents.description: Defines Direct, Pipeline, Router, State Machine, and DAG execution structures plus their cross-cutting production controls.
documents.excerpt: A direct path uses one bounded operation when the information, tools, and output contract are already known. It may still include input validation, one predetermined tool call, an output schema, policy checks, timeouts, and deterministic post-processing. Direct is the strongest baseline when a single operation can sol…
documents.title: Agent execution paths

documents.body:
A DAG represents tasks and directional dependencies without cycles inside one run. Its defining property is dependency, not parallelism or multi-agent organization.

Independent branches may run concurrently when resources allow, but concurrency must be bounded by rate limits, connection pools, model quotas, browser capacity, memory, and cost budgets. Join logic is part of the design: it must define whether all branches are required, whether partial results are acceptable, how timeouts and duplicates are handled, how conflicting evidence is resolved, how provenance is preserved, and whether one failed branch cancels the run.

Retries of a node do not create a semantic graph cycle. Replanning loops usually require an outer state machine, a new DAG run, or another cyclic controller.
documents.description: Defines Direct, Pipeline, Router, State Machine, and DAG execution structures plus their cross-cutting production controls.
documents.excerpt: A DAG represents tasks and directional dependencies without cycles inside one run. Its defining property is dependency, not parallelism or multi-agent organization. Independent branches may run concurrently when resources allow, but concurrency must be bounded by rate limits, connection pools, model quotas, browser ca…
documents.title: Agent execution paths

documents.body:
Event-driven execution defines what starts work and how messages move between producers and consumers. An event may trigger a direct handler, pipeline, state machine, or DAG.

Production events need stable identity, source, type, and trace context. Because distributed delivery is often at least once, side-effecting consumers need idempotency. Designs should also cover out-of-order and late events, poison messages, dead-letter handling, schema evolution, correlation identifiers, backpressure, and replay.
documents.description: Defines Direct, Pipeline, Router, State Machine, and DAG execution structures plus their cross-cutting production controls.
documents.excerpt: Event-driven execution defines what starts work and how messages move between producers and consumers. An event may trigger a direct handler, pipeline, state machine, or DAG. Production events need stable identity, source, type, and trace context. Because distributed delivery is often at least once, side-effecting con…
documents.title: Agent execution paths

documents.body:
Human-in-the-loop is a governed pause and resume point, not a separate reasoning method or topology. It is appropriate for irreversible actions, permission escalation, public publication, financial or destructive operations, production changes, conflicting evidence, policy exceptions, and decisions that remain human-owned.

The reviewer should see the proposed action, supporting evidence, expected impact, risk, reversibility, editable fields, and consequences of approval or rejection. The workflow must persist enough state to resume without repeating earlier side effects and must define edit, reject, timeout, expiry, escalation, and cancellation behavior.
documents.description: Defines Direct, Pipeline, Router, State Machine, and DAG execution structures plus their cross-cutting production controls.
documents.excerpt: Human-in-the-loop is a governed pause and resume point, not a separate reasoning method or topology. It is appropriate for irreversible actions, permission escalation, public publication, financial or destructive operations, production changes, conflicting evidence, policy exceptions, and decisions that remain human-o…
documents.title: Agent execution paths

documents.body:
A pipeline divides work into a predetermined sequence of stages. Each stage may use a model, rule, database, or tool, but the application owns the main route.

A production pipeline should give every stage an explicit input contract, output contract, timeout, error type, and observable result. Optional guarded stages and bounded retries can still fit a pipeline. When waiting, resumability, conditional transitions, and several terminal outcomes become central, the design is better represented as a stateful workflow or state machine.
documents.description: Defines Direct, Pipeline, Router, State Machine, and DAG execution structures plus their cross-cutting production controls.
documents.excerpt: A pipeline divides work into a predetermined sequence of stages. Each stage may use a model, rule, database, or tool, but the application owns the main route. A production pipeline should give every stage an explicit input contract, output contract, timeout, error type, and observable result. Optional guarded stages a…
documents.title: Agent execution paths

documents.body:
Behavior trees organize reusable control and action nodes hierarchically. They are useful for modular, reactive behavior, while state machines may be easier for mixed engineering and operations teams to inspect.

Program generation is an execution technique rather than a peer topology. A model may generate SQL, code, commands, API calls, a DSL, or a workflow definition, but the generated artifact still executes through a direct path, pipeline, state machine, DAG, or combination. Safe execution requires parsing, policy checks, sandboxing, least privilege, allowlists, secret isolation, resource limits, output limits, and tests.
documents.description: Defines Direct, Pipeline, Router, State Machine, and DAG execution structures plus their cross-cutting production controls.
documents.excerpt: Behavior trees organize reusable control and action nodes hierarchically. They are useful for modular, reactive behavior, while state machines may be easier for mixed engineering and operations teams to inspect. Program generation is an execution technique rather than a peer topology. A model may generate SQL, code, c…
documents.title: Agent execution paths

documents.body:
A router selects one or more downstream paths based on task type, source of truth, permissions, sensitivity, latency, cost, risk, required tools, language, tenant, or multiple intents. It does not need to solve the request itself.

Routers may be deterministic, model-based, semantic, or hybrid. Hard permission and safety rules should run before uncertain model decisions. A production router also needs explicit abstention outcomes such as unknown, ambiguous, unsupported, clarification required, or human review. Overrides must be permission-controlled and logged with the selected route, policy version, confidence or decision basis, rejected alternatives, fallback, and risk checks.
documents.description: Defines Direct, Pipeline, Router, State Machine, and DAG execution structures plus their cross-cutting production controls.
documents.excerpt: A router selects one or more downstream paths based on task type, source of truth, permissions, sensitivity, latency, cost, risk, required tools, language, tenant, or multiple intents. It does not need to solve the request itself. Routers may be deterministic, model-based, semantic, or hybrid. Hard permission and safe…
documents.title: Agent execution paths

documents.body:
Use the smallest structure that satisfies the control requirements:

1. Add an event envelope when external events trigger the work.
2. Insert a human or policy gate before high-impact actions.
3. Start with Direct when one bounded operation is enough.
4. Add a Router when requests need different capabilities or sources of truth.
5. Use a Pipeline when stages are known and stable.
6. Use a State Machine when execution needs persisted states, waits, loops, recovery, approvals, or multiple terminal outcomes.
7. Use a DAG when the work is naturally expressed as directional dependencies with fan-out and join.

These choices are composable. A router may start a state machine, one state may launch a DAG, and an output pipeline may format the final result.
documents.description: Defines Direct, Pipeline, Router, State Machine, and DAG execution structures plus their cross-cutting production controls.
documents.excerpt: Use the smallest structure that satisfies the control requirements: 1. Add an event envelope when external events trigger the work. 2. Insert a human or policy gate before high-impact actions. 3. Start with Direct when one bounded operation is enough. 4. Add a Router when requests need different capabilities or source…
documents.title: Agent execution paths

documents.body:
A state machine represents named states and legal transitions selected by guards or conditions. It makes current progress, persisted data, retry limits, waiting states, and terminal outcomes explicit.

Useful terminal outcomes extend beyond success and failure and may include cancelled, partial, expired, or manual resolution required. State machines support bounded recovery, pause and resume, checkpointing, and auditable approvals. A state diagram alone does not guarantee durability or exactly-once effects; those properties depend on persistence, task design, idempotency, and the execution runtime.

documents.excerpt: A state machine represents named states and legal transitions selected by guards or conditions. It makes current progress, persisted data, retry limits, waiting states, and terminal outcomes explicit. Useful terminal outcomes extend beyond success and failure and may include cancelled, partial, expired, or manual reso…

documents.excerpt: Adaptive planning revises the remaining plan when execution evidence invalidates a material assumption. It should not rewrite the whole plan after every observation. Valid replan triggers include: - a critical premise becomes false; - required data or a capability is unavailable; - a dependency changes; - the goal or…
documents.title: Agent decision and planning strategies

documents.body: An agent system's outer execution structure and its node-level decision strategy are separate architectural layers. A State Machine may control legal progress while one state uses bounded ReAct, a Planner may create work that later executes as a DAG, and a fixed Pipeline may contain a planning or verification stage. Production reviews should name both layers instead of forcing the entire system under one pattern label.

documents.excerpt: An agent system's outer execution structure and its node-level decision strategy are separate architectural layers. P…
documents.title: Agent decision and planning strategies

documents.description: Defines fixed logic, bounded ReAct, Plan-and-Execute, adaptive replanning, hierarchical planning, HTN, and their production control boundaries.
documents.excerpt: ReAct-style execution chooses the next local action after observing the result of the previous action. Its useful production form is a closed loop: 1. read the current goal and state; 2. select an allowed action; 3. execute a tool; 4. normalise the observation; 5. update progress; 6. complete, continue, or escalate. T…
documents.title: Agent decision and planning strategies

documents.body:
Fixed decision logic keeps the next step under application control when the sequence is already known. The model may perform work inside a stage, but it does not redesign the workflow.

This is a strong default for stable ingestion, extraction, transformation, validation, approval procedures, and other tasks with repeatable contracts. Its advantages are predictable cost and latency, straightforward tests, explicit permissions, reproducibility, and clear failure attribution.

A fixed flow still needs typed responses for unmodelled conditions. When a required source disappears or a dependency fails, the system should use an approved fallback, mark data unavailable, request missing authority, stop with a typed failure, or escalate. Deterministic control is not an immature design; autonomy should be added only where predetermined rules are genuinely insufficient.
documents.description: Defines fixed logic, bounded ReAct, Plan-and-Execute, adaptive replanning, hierarchical planning, HTN, and their production control boundaries.
documents.excerpt: Fixed decision logic keeps the next step under application control when the sequence is already known. The model may perform work inside a stage, but it does not redesign the workflow. This is a strong default for stable ingestion, extraction, transformation, validation, approval procedures, and other tasks with repea…
documents.title: Agent decision and planning strategies

documents.body:
Goals and policies affect every decision strategy but are not peer planning algorithms.

A goal defines the desired state or measurable acceptance condition. Weak goals such as "improve the system" invite drift. Strong goals specify observable completion, including tests, evidence, output fields, or terminal status.

A policy determines whether a proposed action may proceed, requires approval, or must be denied. Policies may cover tools, permissions, data access, cost, risk, privacy, network access, reversibility, and human approval.

Important policy enforcement should live in application or infrastructure controls such as scoped credentials, network restrictions, sandboxes, tool allowlists, spending limits, and approval gates. The agent should not be the sole authority deciding whether its own restriction applies.
documents.description: Defines fixed logic, bounded ReAct, Plan-and-Execute, adaptive replanning, hierarchical planning, HTN, and their production control boundaries.
documents.excerpt: Goals and policies affect every decision strategy but are not peer planning algorithms. A goal defines the desired state or measurable acceptance condition. Weak goals such as "improve the system" invite drift. Strong goals specify observable completion, including tests, evidence, output fields, or terminal status. A…
documents.title: Agent decision and planning strategies

documents.body:
Hierarchical planning decomposes a large goal into subgoals and executable tasks at several levels. It helps isolate context, delegate ownership, manage independent branches, and keep upper levels focused on outcomes rather than tool calls.

Hierarchy also introduces risks: duplicated work, inconsistent assumptions, intent loss during hand-offs, incompatible output formats, and completion of every child task without completion of the parent goal.

A production hierarchy therefore needs parent and child completion contracts, shared terminology and facts, provenance, dependency management, integration ownership, and cross-subgoal verification.
documents.description: Defines fixed logic, bounded ReAct, Plan-and-Execute, adaptive replanning, hierarchical planning, HTN, and their production control boundaries.
documents.excerpt: Hierarchical planning decomposes a large goal into subgoals and executable tasks at several levels. It helps isolate context, delegate ownership, manage independent branches, and keep upper levels focused on outcomes rather than tool calls. Hierarchy also introduces risks: duplicated work, inconsistent assumptions, in…
documents.title: Agent decision and planning strategies

documents.body:
Hierarchical Task Network planning is a formal approach that refines compound tasks through domain methods until executable primitive tasks remain.

- A compound task requires decomposition.
- A method defines an allowed domain-specific decomposition and its conditions.
- A primitive task can be executed directly by the runtime.

HTN is useful where reusable procedures, consistency, auditability, and allowed methods matter more than open-ended creativity. Its strengths depend on the quality and freshness of the domain model. Building and maintaining that model is expensive, unmodelled tasks remain difficult, and stale methods reproduce stale behaviour.

LLMs and HTN can be combined. An LLM can interpret natural-language intent and extract parameters, an HTN planner can select governed methods, the execution system can run permitted primitive tasks, and an LLM can handle language-heavy steps or explain results. The model handles ambiguity while the HTN layer constrains procedure.
documents.description: Defines fixed logic, bounded ReAct, Plan-and-Execute, adaptive replanning, hierarchical planning, HTN, and their production control boundaries.
documents.excerpt: Hierarchical Task Network planning is a formal approach that refines compound tasks through domain methods until executable primitive tasks remain. - A compound task requires decomposition. - A method defines an allowed domain-specific decomposition and its conditions. - A primitive task can be executed directly by th…

documents.description: Defines fixed logic, bounded ReAct, Plan-and-Execute, adaptive replanning, hierarchical planning, HTN, and their production control boundaries.
documents.excerpt: Plan-and-Execute creates a global task structure before carrying out individual steps. It is best treated as a pattern family rather than one canonical algorithm. A useful plan exposes requirements, ordering, dependencies, delegation, expected outputs, and completion state. It is appropriate for long research, multi-d…
documents.title: Agent decision and planning strategies

documents.body:
Validation strength should match task risk.

Natural-language review can detect missing requirements or weak sequencing in low-risk work. Deterministic validation should check required fields, dependency references, acyclic dependencies, allowed tools, budget totals, preconditions, and output schemas.

Simulation or dry runs are appropriate before irreversible actions. When actions, preconditions, effects, and constraints have a formal model, an external planner or solver may provide stronger feasibility guarantees than free-form generation.

Fluent plan generation is not proof that a plan is valid.

documents.excerpt: A robust production architecture commonly combines the strategies: 1. a Planner creates a versioned plan with dependencies, budgets, outputs, and approval boundaries; 2. a State Machine persists progress and selects a ready step; 3. a bounded Executor uses fixed logic or ReAct within the step contract; 4. a Verifier c…

documents.excerpt: Use the least flexible mechanism that can reliably complete the task: 1. keep deterministic work in fixed logic; 2. add bounded ReAct when the next useful action depends on an observation; 3. add Plan-and-Execute when global coverage, ordering, dependencies, or visible progress matter; 4. add adaptive replanning only…

Tool results s…
documents.title: Agent decision and planning strategies

documents.body:
The quartz lantern protocol is the internal acceptance phrase for the M3 candidate pipeline.

A source commit is eligible for candidate publication only when the Builder checks out the exact reviewed SHA, rebuilds the release twice with identical artifacts, uploads immutable objects to Cloudflare R2, loads the candidate through Runtime integrity verification, returns an authorized result with citations, and denies the same internal material to a public audience.

Candidate publication must leave the `production` channel pointer byte-for-byte unchanged. Production promotion remains a separate reviewed operation.
documents.description: Defines the private acceptance gates between reviewed source and a promotable Knowledge Engine release.
documents.excerpt: The quartz lantern protocol is the internal acceptance phrase for the M3 candidate pipeline. A source commit is eligible for candidate publication only when the Builder checks out the exact reviewed SHA, rebuilds the release twice with identical artifacts, uploads immutable objects to Cloudflare R2, loads the candidat…

documents.body: A later operator should be able to reconstruct the run from the authoritative state and attached evidence without trusting an unbounded conversation transcript.

documents.body: The authority records what has been admitted, attempted, observed, accepted, deferred, failed, or cancelled. It prevents chat memory, ad hoc scripts, or transient tool output from becoming the only record of execution truth.

documents.body: Completion is a state transition owned by the harness or by an explicitly named reviewer. A model may propose completion, but it should not be the sole authority for acceptance.
documents.description: Defines the authority that accepts or rejects a terminal candidate against contract and evidence.

documents.body: A completion gate is the authority that decides whether a terminal candidate satisfies the task contract, evidence requirements, and safety boundaries.
documents.description: Defines the authority that accepts or rejects a terminal candidate against contract and evidence.

documents.body: The gate may accept, reject, request repair, defer for human decision, or mark the task blocked. It should rely on durable evidence rather than a model claim that the work is done.

documents.title: Durable Thread State

documents.body: Durable thread state is persistent interaction state that survives client disconnects, spans turns, and preserves enough context to resume without repeating unsafe side effects.
documents.description: Defines persistent interaction state that survives disconnects and spans turns.

documents.title: Durable Thread State

documents.body: Durability supports pause, resume, crash recovery, audit, and handoff. It does not by itself authorize a side effect; authority still comes from the harness and task contract.

documents.body: Drift should be handled by re-contracting the task, recording a new decision, or stopping. It should not be hidden inside implementation details.

documents.body: Drift appears when the run optimizes for a different outcome, adds unapproved scope, changes gates to force completion, treats diagnostics as product work, or uses later observations to rewrite the original authority.

documents.body: A harness is not the model itself and is not the generated answer. It is the surrounding product and runtime contract that decides what work may start, what evidence is required, which actions are allowed, when state becomes authoritative, and when the run must stop.
documents.description: Defines the governed execution boundary around an LLM or agent runtime.
documents.excerpt: A harness is not the model itself and is not the generated answer. It is the surrounding product and runtime contract that decides what work may start, what evidence is required, which actions are allowed, when state becomes authoritative, and when the run must stop.
documents.title: Harness

documents.body: A harness is the governed execution and control system around an LLM or agent runtime. It defines the request boundary, task contract, run authority, completion gate, steering surface, verification boundary, and stopping policy that make autonomous work reviewable and recoverable.
documents.description: Defines the governed execution boundary around an LLM or agent runtime.
documents.excerpt: A harness is the governed execution and control system around an LLM or agent runtime. It defines the request boundary, task contract, run authority, completion gate, steering surface, verification boundary, and stopping policy that make autonomous work reviewable and recoverable.
documents.title: Harness

documents.body: A useful harness makes proposals, tool calls, observations, approvals, failures, retries, and terminal outcomes visible as governed state. It gives an operator a stable place to inspect and steer work without granting the agent unrestricted authority.
documents.description: Defines the governed execution boundary around an LLM or agent runtime.
documents.excerpt: A useful harness makes proposals, tool calls, observations, approvals, failures, retries, and terminal outcomes visible as governed state. It gives an operator a stable place to inspect and steer work without granting the agent unrestricted authority.

documents.body: Verification may pass, fail, request repair, require human review, or trigger governed defer. Its result should be recorded as evidence rather than left as an implicit judgment in a model response.

documents.body: A highly harnessable workflow has stable inputs, explicit authority, deterministic or inspectable effects, typed failures, replayable evidence, and clear completion gates. Low harnessability calls for smaller scopes, stronger adapters, or human review before automation.
documents.description: Defines the measurable fitness of a task or environment for governed harness execution.

documents.body: Harnessability is the measurable fitness of a task, environment, or tool surface for governed harness execution.
documents.description: Defines the measurable fitness of a task or environment for governed harness execution.
documents.excerpt: Harnessability is the measurable fitness of a task, environment, or tool surface for governed harness execution.
documents.title: Harnessability

documents.body: In this Source adoption, harnessability is not a general quality word. It refers specifically to whether work can be bounded by a task contract, observed through durable state, verified with evidence, stopped safely, and resumed or handed off without relying on hidden chat memory.
documents.description: Defines the measurable fitness of a task or environment for governed harness execution.

It refers specifically to whether work can be bounded by a task contract, observed through durable state, verified with evidence, stopped safely, and resumed or handed off without relying on hidden chat memory.

documents.body: A headless harness service is a UI-independent execution core that exposes harness operations through a stable protocol rather than tying them to one chat client or browser surface.
documents.description: Defines a UI-independent harness execution core shared by multiple clients through a stable protocol.
documents.excerpt: A headless harness service is a UI-independent execution core that exposes harness operations through a stable protocol rather than tying them to one chat client or browser surface.
documents.title: Headless Harness Service

documents.body: Headless operation lets a CLI, web app, background worker, or review console share one governed execution record instead of creating disconnected task memories.
documents.description: Defines a UI-independent harness execution core shared by multiple clients through a stable protocol.
documents.excerpt: Headless operation lets a CLI, web app, background worker, or review console share one governed execution record instead of creating disconnected task memories.
documents.title: Headless Harness Service

documents.body: The service can own task admission, durable thread state, turn execution, tool authorization, observations, evidence, and completion transitions while multiple clients render or steer the same underlying run.

documents.body: The item turn thread protocol is a harness interaction model that separates typed interaction items, pausable work turns, and durable threads.
documents.description: Defines a protocol model separating interaction items, pausable work turns, and durable threads.
documents.excerpt: The item turn thread protocol is a harness interaction model that separates typed interaction items, pausable work turns, and durable threads.
documents.title: Item Turn Thread Protocol

documents.body: Items carry messages, tool proposals, approvals, observations, evidence, or status updates. Turns describe bounded work units. Threads persist the run context and connect turns over time.
documents.description: Defines a protocol model separating interaction items, pausable work turns, and durable threads.

documents.body: The boundary should normalize the request, validate required fields, attach identity and authority context, reject unsupported operations, and prevent ambiguous instructions from entering the execution core as if they were approved work.

documents.body:
- Agent architecture patterns can coexist because they operate at different architectural layers rather than competing for one role.
- A practical agent architecture review can be organized across six lenses: execution path, decision and planning, reasoning and search, verification and recovery, agent organization, and state and memory.
- The six-dimensional map is an engineering model optimized for design and production work, not a universal academic taxonomy.
documents.description: LLM agent architectures should be reviewed across separate engineering dimensions because multiple patterns can coexist at different layers of one production system.

documents.body:
`knowledge-source` is the human-authored source of truth. Generated indexes, release manifests, runtime caches, R2 snapshots, and Oracle deployment state are outputs rather than authoring inputs.

Every source change must pass deterministic validation and review before the Builder consumes an exact commit SHA. Candidate publication and production promotion remain separate gates.

See the [bundle index](../index.md).
documents.description: Defines the reviewable boundary between human-authored source and generated runtime releases.
documents.excerpt: `knowledge-source` is the human-authored source of truth. Generated indexes, release manifests, runtime caches, R2 snapshots, and Oracle deployment state are outputs rather than authoring inputs. Every source change must pass deterministic validation and review before the Builder consumes an exact commit SHA. Candidat…

documents.body: A stopping policy prevents repeated observation, blind reruns, and open-ended repair loops by requiring evidence-rich attempts and a governed defer path when the current run cannot safely finish.
documents.description: Defines rules for continuing, waiting, completing, failing, cancelling, or deferring execution.
documents.excerpt: A stopping policy prevents repeated observation, blind reruns, and open-ended repair loops by requiring evidence-rich attempts and a governed defer path when the current run cannot safely finish.

documents.body: The contract should name the requested result, allowed actions, forbidden mutations, required proof, acceptance criteria, budget or attempt limits, escalation conditions, and the authority that can mark the work complete.

documents.excerpt: The contract should name the requested result, allowed actions, forbidden mutations, required proof, acceptance criteria, budget or attempt limits, escalation conditions, and the authority that can mark the work complete.

documents.body: Goal drift is detectable only when there is a stable contract to compare against. Changes to the contract should be versioned or explicitly re-authorized instead of being absorbed silently into the run.
